title: Listening - Part 3: School Trips
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a school trip they particularly enjoyed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker appreciated most about the trip. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a school trip they particularly enjoyed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker appreciated most about the trip. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five students talk about a school trip they particularly enjoyed.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: We’d been studying rivers in geography, drawing diagrams of erosion and measuring things from photographs. I could repeat the definitions for a test, but they didn't mean very much to me. On our trip to a valley, [19]we stood beside the river and identified the bends, stones and banks we had only seen in textbooks, so suddenly the whole topic made practical sense.[/19] It rained almost constantly and my boots filled with water, yet I understood more in one afternoon than in several classroom lessons.||Speaker 2 Man: Our art class visited a gallery displaying portraits. Usually we walk around and write short notes, which I don’t find very memorable. This time a working artist met us there. [20]She explained how painters use tiny changes in light and expression, then asked us to study one face carefully; I noticed details I would never have seen without her guidance.[/20] Back at school, I used one of her techniques in my own drawing, and for once I was genuinely pleased with the result.||Speaker 3 Woman: I wasn’t looking forward to our camping trip because our class tends to argue whenever we have to make decisions together. On the second day, we were given a route to follow across the hills and several practical problems to solve. [21]To my surprise, everyone listened to one another, divided up the jobs sensibly and got the whole group back to camp ahead of schedule.[/21] Reaching the end felt better than winning a competition, because it showed we could actually depend on each other.||Speaker 4 Man: Our history trip took place at a restored nineteenth-century village. I expected all of us to complete the same worksheet, as usual. Instead, there were several activities, including examining clothes, interviewing staff in character and studying household objects. [22]We were free to select whichever investigation interested us most, and I chose to explore how children were educated because it related to a project I was already doing.[/22] Having control over what I studied made me much more involved than on most school visits.||Speaker 5 Woman: The destination of our science trip was an island nature reserve. I was interested in the birds, but I had visited wildlife parks before, so that wasn't completely new. [23]What made the day memorable was getting there in a small sailing boat rather than taking a coach, and helping to adjust the sails while we crossed the bay.[/23] Some students felt sick, but I loved every minute. By the time we reached the island, I already felt we’d had an adventure.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: We’d been studying rivers in geography, drawing diagrams of erosion and measuring things from photographs. I could repeat the definitions for a test, but they didn't mean very much to me. On our trip to a valley, we stood beside the river and identified the bends, stones and banks we had only seen in textbooks, so suddenly the whole topic made practical sense. It rained almost constantly and my boots filled with water, yet I understood more in one afternoon than in several classroom lessons.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: Our art class visited a gallery displaying portraits. Usually we walk around and write short notes, which I don’t find very memorable. This time a working artist met us there. She explained how painters use tiny changes in light and expression, then asked us to study one face carefully; I noticed details I would never have seen without her guidance. Back at school, I used one of her techniques in my own drawing, and for once I was genuinely pleased with the result.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I wasn’t looking forward to our camping trip because our class tends to argue whenever we have to make decisions together. On the second day, we were given a route to follow across the hills and several practical problems to solve. To my surprise, everyone listened to one another, divided up the jobs sensibly and got the whole group back to camp ahead of schedule. Reaching the end felt better than winning a competition, because it showed we could actually depend on each other.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: Our history trip took place at a restored nineteenth-century village. I expected all of us to complete the same worksheet, as usual. Instead, there were several activities, including examining clothes, interviewing staff in character and studying household objects. We were free to select whichever investigation interested us most, and I chose to explore how children were educated because it related to a project I was already doing. Having control over what I studied made me much more involved than on most school visits.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: The destination of our science trip was an island nature reserve. I was interested in the birds, but I had visited wildlife parks before, so that wasn't completely new. What made the day memorable was getting there in a small sailing boat rather than taking a coach, and helping to adjust the sails while we crossed the bay. Some students felt sick, but I loved every minute. By the time we reached the island, I already felt we’d had an adventure.

options.text: being allowed to choose their own task
getting to know a teacher better
seeing a subject in a real setting
trying food they had never tasted

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Seeing river features in the landscape made the geography topic understandable for the speaker.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker valued receiving guidance about portraits from a professional artist.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker was impressed by how effectively the class cooperated during a group challenge.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker enjoyed being able to choose the aspect of village life to investigate.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The journey by sailing boat was the most special aspect of the speaker's trip.
